# RunAgents — Full Documentation

> RunAgents is a platform for deploying and orchestrating AI agents with secure, policy-driven access to external tools and services. It handles identity propagation, credential injection, approval workflows, and access control so agent developers can focus on logic instead of security plumbing.

The same governed runtime can sit behind many interfaces, including a web app, WhatsApp, Slack, an internal portal, or a custom client. RunAgents keeps execution, identity propagation, policy enforcement, approval workflows, and outbound tool security consistent regardless of the user-facing surface.

---

## What's New

Recent updates are published in the public `What's New` section:

- Scoped approvals for governed writes
- Clearer approval-versus-consent operations UX
- Google Workspace calendar writes under policy control
- Stronger pause-and-resume behavior for messaging surfaces such as WhatsApp

See:

- https://docs.runagents.io/whats-new/
- https://docs.runagents.io/whats-new/releases/2026-04-09-cli-v1-2-0-governance-and-operations/
- https://docs.runagents.io/whats-new/releases/2026-04-09-scoped-approvals-console-messaging/

---

## Architecture

RunAgents processes every agent interaction through three stages: Ingress, Runtime, and Egress.

The initiating surface can live anywhere: a traditional web app, WhatsApp, Slack, an internal operator UI, or a custom client.

```
Client App --JWT--> [RunAgents Ingress] --X-End-User-ID--> Agent
                                                              |
                                              +---------------+
                                              v               v
                                         [LLM Gateway]   [Tool Proxy]
                                              |               |
                                              v               v
                                         Model Provider   External API
                                         (OpenAI, etc.)   (Stripe, etc.)
```

### Stage 1: Ingress (Client to Agent)

Client applications authenticate users via JWT. The platform validates the JWT signature against the identity provider's JWKS endpoint, extracts user identity from a configurable claim (e.g., email, sub), injects it as the X-End-User-ID header, and enforces domain-level access control.

Your agent never sees the raw JWT. It receives the verified user identity as a simple HTTP header.

### Stage 2: Runtime (Agent Executes)

The agent runs with everything injected:
- System prompt loaded from agent configuration
- Tool URLs as environment variables (e.g., TOOL_STRIPE_URL)
- LLM Gateway URL for all model inference
- Model settings per role (LLM_MODEL_DEFAULT, LLM_PROVIDER_DEFAULT)

No API keys appear in agent code — the platform manages all credentials.

### Stage 3: Egress (Agent to Tool)

Every outbound call from an agent is intercepted. The platform:
1. Identifies the target tool by matching the destination host
2. Verifies the agent's service identity
3. Evaluates access policies (allow / deny+approval required / deny)
4. Enforces capability-level allow-lists (method + path)
5. Injects authentication credentials (API key, OAuth2 token, AWS SigV4)
6. Forwards the X-End-User-ID header to the downstream API

### Key Design Principles

- Zero-trust by default: no outbound request reaches an external service without policy evaluation
- Identity at every hop: user identity flows from client to agent to tool
- Credentials never in code: managed by the platform, injected at egress
- Least privilege: agents only access explicitly bound tools with operation-level granularity
- Human-in-the-loop: high-risk operations require explicit admin approval

---

## Quickstart

Deploy your first agent in 5 minutes using the RunAgents console.

Prerequisites: A RunAgents account. Sign up at try.runagents.io.

1. Log in to the console
2. Click "Deploy Hello World Agent" on the dashboard
3. The platform seeds starter resources (Echo Tool + Playground LLM)
4. Review the deploy wizard — everything is pre-wired
5. Click Deploy

The platform analyzes the sample code, wires tools and LLM, creates access policies, and deploys the agent. The agent is live and all requests to the Echo Tool are automatically policy-checked, credential-enriched, and identity-forwarded.

For a more production-shaped starting point, use the Agent Catalog instead of starting only with Hello World.

---

## Agent Catalog

The Agent Catalog gives teams maintained starting points for real workflows.

Use it when you want:

- a richer example than Hello World
- policy, approval, and OAuth behavior to be part of the starting point
- a production-shaped blueprint instead of a pure demo

One strong example is the Google Workspace assistant, which combines Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Docs, Sheets, Tasks, and Keep with policy-controlled writes and delegated-user OAuth.

---

## Writing Agents

Six patterns from SDK-based to framework-based:

### Using the RunAgents SDK (Recommended)

```python
from runagents import Agent

agent = Agent()  # reads TOOL_URL_*, LLM_GATEWAY_URL, LLM_MODEL from env

def handler(request, ctx):
    message = request["message"]
    result = agent.chat(message)
    return result["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
```

Install: `pip install runagents`
Scaffold: `runagents init my-agent && cd my-agent && runagents dev`

### Tier 1: Hello World (No Custom Code)

```python
import os, json, urllib.request

TOOL_URL = os.environ.get("TOOL_URL_ECHO_TOOL", "http://echo-tool:8080")
LLM_URL = os.environ.get("LLM_GATEWAY_URL", "http://llm-gateway:8080/v1/chat/completions")
LLM_MODEL = os.environ.get("LLM_MODEL", "gpt-4o-mini")

def call_echo_tool(message):
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        f"{TOOL_URL}/echo",
        data=json.dumps({"message": message}).encode(),
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
        return json.loads(resp.read())

def ask_llm(prompt):
    body = {"model": LLM_MODEL, "messages": [
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": prompt},
    ]}
    req = urllib.request.Request(LLM_URL, data=json.dumps(body).encode(),
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"})
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
        return json.loads(resp.read())["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    echo_result = call_echo_tool("Hello from my first agent!")
    answer = ask_llm("Summarize what RunAgents does in one sentence.")
```

Deploy: `runagents deploy --name hello-world --file agent.py --policy echo-read-policy`

### Tier 2: Custom Handler

```python
def handler(request, context):
    # context.tools = {"tool-name": "http://..."}, context.llm_url, context.model
    message = request["message"]
    return {"response": "..."}
```

### Tier 2: OpenAI SDK

```python
import openai  # OPENAI_BASE_URL auto-set to LLM Gateway
client = openai.OpenAI()
response = client.chat.completions.create(model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=[...], tools=[...])
```

### Tier 2: LangChain

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_openai_tools_agent
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
executor = AgentExecutor(agent=agent, tools=[get_weather, calculate])
```

### Tier 2: LangGraph

```python
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END
workflow = StateGraph(AgentState)
workflow.add_node("model", call_model)
workflow.add_node("tools", call_tools)
graph = workflow.compile()
```

All patterns get the same platform features: identity propagation, policy enforcement, token injection, and mesh routing.

---

## Deploying Agents

Three-step wizard: Upload, Wire, Deploy.

### Upload
Upload Python source files. The platform analyzes code to detect tool calls, LLM usage, secrets, outbound destinations, required packages, and entry point.

### Wire
Map detected dependencies to registered platform resources:
- Detected tools → registered tools (dropdown selector)
- Detected LLM usage → registered model providers
- Set agent name and system prompt
- Configure access control (Open or Authenticated)

### Deploy
Review summary and click Deploy. The platform builds a container image (if needed), creates the agent with tool bindings, and starts it.

### Injected Environment Variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| SYSTEM_PROMPT | Agent's system prompt |
| LLM_GATEWAY_URL | LLM Gateway chat completions URL |
| LLM_MODEL | Model name |
| TOOL_URL_{NAME} | Base URL for each tool |
| TOOL_DEFINITIONS_JSON | OpenAI-format tool definitions |
| TOOL_ROUTES_JSON | Function name to HTTP route mapping |
| OPENAI_BASE_URL | Auto-set to LLM Gateway |
| OPENAI_API_KEY | Auto-set to "platform-managed" |

---

## Registering Tools

Tools are external APIs your agents call. Register a tool to tell the platform how to reach it, authenticate, and control access.

### Configuration
- Name, Description
- Location: External (SaaS) or Internal
- Base URL, Port, Protocol

### Authentication Types
- None: no auth added
- API Key: injected as header or query param
- OAuth2: full flow with token refresh and per-user consent

### Access Control Modes
- Open: any bound agent gets automatic access
- Restricted: requires explicit policy binding
- Critical: requires JIT admin approval for each access

### Capabilities
Operation-level allow-lists (method + path). When declared, only matching operations are allowed. Empty = passthrough.

---

## Deploy API

POST /api/deploy — deploy agents programmatically.

Request body fields:
- agent_name (required): unique agent name
- source_files: map of filename to source code
- image: pre-built container image URI
- system_prompt: LLM context
- required_tools: names of existing tools
- tools_to_create: new tools to register [{name, base_url, description, auth_type}]
- llm_configs: [{provider, model, role}]
- requirements: pip requirements
- entry_point: main file

Example:
```bash
curl -X POST https://api.runagents.io/api/deploy \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $RUNAGENTS_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "agent_name": "payment-agent",
    "source_files": {"agent.py": "import requests\nimport openai\n..."},
    "required_tools": ["stripe-api"],
    "llm_configs": [{"role": "chat", "provider": "openai", "model": "gpt-4o-mini"}]
  }'
```

POST /api/starter-kit — create demo echo-tool and playground-llm (idempotent).

---

## Python SDK

Install: `pip install runagents` (zero dependencies, Python 3.10+)
MCP server: `pip install runagents[mcp]`
Local dev hot-reload: `pip install runagents[dev]`
PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/runagents/ (v1.2.1)

### Client — manage platform resources

```python
from runagents import Client

client = Client()  # reads ~/.runagents/config.json + env vars
# or: Client(endpoint=..., api_key=..., namespace=...)

agents = client.agents.list()           # → list[Agent]
agent  = client.agents.get(ns, name)    # → Agent
result = client.agents.deploy(name, source_files={"agent.py": "..."}, required_tools=["stripe-api"], llm_configs=[{"provider":"openai","model":"gpt-4o-mini","role":"default"}])

tools  = client.tools.list()            # → list[Tool]
client.tools.create(name, base_url, description, auth_type)

runs   = client.runs.list(agent="name") # → list[Run]
events = client.runs.events(run_id)     # → list[Event]

approvals = client.approvals.list()
client.approvals.approve(request_id)
client.approvals.reject(request_id)

result = client.analyze({"agent.py": code})  # → AnalysisResult
client.seed_starter_kit()
```

### Agent — write agent code

```python
from runagents import Agent

agent = Agent()
# Reads from env: SYSTEM_PROMPT, LLM_GATEWAY_URL, LLM_MODEL, TOOL_URL_{NAME}

# Call a platform tool (mesh handles auth)
result = agent.call_tool("stripe-api", "/v1/charges", {"amount": 1000}, method="POST")

# Chat via LLM gateway (your API key never in code)
response = agent.chat("Summarise this", tools=[...], history=[...])
```

### @tool decorator

```python
from runagents import tool

@tool
def calculate(expression: str) -> str:
    """Evaluate a math expression."""
    return str(eval(expression))

@tool(name="weather-lookup", description="Get weather for a city")
def get_weather(city: str) -> dict: ...
```

### CLI

```bash
runagents init my-agent   # scaffold project (agent.py, runagents.yaml, CLAUDE.md, .mcp.json, ...)
runagents dev             # local dev server :8080 + mock tools :9090
runagents dev --watch     # with hot-reload (requires pip install runagents[dev])
```

Config: env vars > ~/.runagents/config.json > defaults
RUNAGENTS_ENDPOINT, RUNAGENTS_API_KEY, RUNAGENTS_NAMESPACE, RUNAGENTS_ASSISTANT_MODE

---

## CLI Reference

Install: `pip install runagents` (recommended — includes CLI, SDK, and runtime) or `npm install -g @runagents/cli` or `brew install runagents-io/tap/runagents` or `curl -fsSL https://runagents-releases.s3.amazonaws.com/cli/install.sh | sh`

### Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| runagents deploy | Deploy agent from source files, drafts, or artifacts |
| runagents catalog | Discover and deploy maintained catalog agents |
| runagents policies | Manage policy rules and approval routing |
| runagents identity-providers | Manage end-user identity providers |
| runagents approval-connectors | Manage approval delivery integrations |
| runagents context export | Export workspace state for external assistants |
| runagents agents list | List agents |
| runagents agents get | Get agent details |
| runagents tools list | List tools |
| runagents tools create | Register a tool |
| runagents models list | List model providers |
| runagents runs list | List runs |
| runagents runs get | Get run details |
| runagents runs timeline | Show a readable per-run timeline |
| runagents runs wait | Block until a run reaches a terminal state |
| runagents runs export | Export a run with events for debugging |
| runagents approvals list | List access requests |
| runagents approvals approve | Approve access request |
| runagents analyze | Analyze source code |
| runagents starter-kit | Seed demo resources |
| runagents config | Manage CLI configuration |

### Configuration

```bash
runagents config set endpoint https://YOUR_WORKSPACE.try.runagents.io
runagents config set api-key YOUR_KEY
runagents config set namespace default
```

Config stored at ~/.runagents/config.json. Environment variables override: RUNAGENTS_ENDPOINT, RUNAGENTS_API_KEY, RUNAGENTS_NAMESPACE.

---

## MCP Server

Install: `pip install runagents[mcp]`

14 tools: list_agents, get_agent, list_tools, list_models, list_runs, get_run_events, export_context, analyze_code, deploy_agent, create_tool, validate_plan, apply_plan, approve_request, seed_starter_kit.

Claude Code config:
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "runagents": {
      "command": "runagents-mcp",
      "env": {
        "RUNAGENTS_ENDPOINT": "https://YOUR_WORKSPACE.try.runagents.io",
        "RUNAGENTS_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
        "RUNAGENTS_NAMESPACE": "default"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Public skills library: https://docs.runagents.io/cli/skills/

Use it when you want reusable workflows for Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, and similar assistants across catalog deployment, tool onboarding, approvals, run debugging, or anywhere-interface design such as WhatsApp and Slack.

---

## Identity Propagation

User identity flows end-to-end: Client App (JWT) → RunAgents Ingress (validates, extracts claim) → Agent (receives X-End-User-ID header) → Tool (receives same header). The agent never handles raw JWTs. The downstream tool knows which user the agent acts on behalf of.

## Policy Model

Policies define resource URN patterns with allow/deny rules. PolicyBindings link identities (ServiceAccount, User, Group) to Policies. At runtime, the ext-authz service evaluates bindings for the requesting agent's ServiceAccount against the target tool.

## OAuth & Consent

For tools using OAuth2 with an authorization URL, RunAgents manages per-user consent. On first access: agent gets 403 with authorization_url → user is redirected to provider consent screen → after granting, callback stores per-user refresh token → subsequent requests use the stored token automatically.

---

## Pricing

RunAgents offers a free trial at try.runagents.io. Contact try@runagents.io for pricing details.
